3-social-studies-civics-and-government-c.title: Citizenship & Rights
3-social-studies-civics-and-government-c.summary: Students use a kid-friendly rights poster and a short First Amendment paraphrase to distinguish a right, a rule, and a responsibility.
objectives: Identify one right named in a civic source
Explain how a responsibility helps protect rights in a community
Use a detail from a short source to support an answer
3-social-studies-civics-and-government-c.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show two cards: one names a right such as speaking respectfully, and one names a school rule such as walking in the hallway. Ask students how the cards are different.
outline.direct_instruction: Introduce rights as protections people have and responsibilities as actions that help a community work. Read a short paraphrase of the First Amendment and model how to notice what the source says directly.
outline.guided_practice: Read a simple citizenship poster together. Sort examples into rights, rules, and responsibilities. Ask which words in the source helped students decide.
outline.independent_practice: Students read a short school-community scenario and explain which right is involved and what responsibility would help protect it.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students name a right from the source, tell how it differs from a rule, and point to the evidence they used?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete the sentence: One right I noticed was ____, and one responsibility connected to it is ____.

3-social-studies-civics-and-government-l.title: Local/State Government
3-social-studies-civics-and-government-l.summary: Students use a town-services chart and a state map to learn which problems are handled locally and which are handled by state government.
objectives: Identify a job of local government
Identify a job of state government
Use details from a chart or map to justify an answer
3-social-studies-civics-and-government-l.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask who fixes a neighborhood stop sign and who helps run state parks. Let students guess before showing a local-versus-state services chart.
outline.direct_instruction: Explain that local government handles community needs close to home, while state government makes decisions for the whole state. Model how to read headings and symbols in a services chart and state map.
outline.guided_practice: Work through a chart with examples such as schools, roads, parks, and libraries. Discuss which level of government fits each example and why.
outline.independent_practice: Students sort community problems into local or state categories and cite one chart detail or map clue for each answer.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students explain why a job belongs to local or state government instead of only naming a category?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: A problem local government can solve is ____. A problem state government can solve is ____.

3-social-studies-economics-and-financial.title: Markets (intro)
3-social-studies-economics-and-financial.summary: Students read a farmers market price board and notice how people buy and sell goods in a market.
objectives: Explain what a market is
Identify buyers and sellers in a simple source
Use a price list to answer basic questions
3-social-studies-economics-and-financial.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a picture of a farmers market with a sign listing apple, bread, and flower prices. Ask what people are doing in the picture.
outline.direct_instruction: Define a market as a place where buyers and sellers exchange goods or services. Model how to read a price board and notice who is selling and who is buying.
outline.guided_practice: Use the market sign to answer questions such as which item costs more and what a family could buy with a set amount of money.
outline.independent_practice: Students read a new mini price list and explain what is being sold, who might buy it, and how the source helped them decide.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students explain how a market helps buyers and sellers instead of only naming prices?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write one sentence explaining what a market is and name one thing a buyer and seller each do there.

3-social-studies-economics-and-financial.title: Producers/Consumers
3-social-studies-economics-and-financial.summary: Students use a product label, receipt, and picture cards to tell the difference between a producer and a consumer.
objectives: Define producer and consumer
Identify producers and consumers in a simple scenario
Use clues from a label or receipt to explain an answer
3-social-studies-economics-and-financial.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Hold up a loaf of bread and a store receipt. Ask who helped make the bread and who bought it.
outline.direct_instruction: Teach that producers make or provide goods and services, while consumers buy or use them. Model how a label and receipt show clues about both roles.
outline.guided_practice: Sort picture cards of bakers, cashiers, families, and shoppers into producer and consumer groups. Ask students what evidence supports each choice.
outline.independent_practice: Students read a short shopping scene and identify the producers and consumers involved in the exchange.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students explain why one person counts as a producer and another as a consumer in the same situation?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: A producer is ____. A consumer is ____. In my example, the evidence was ____.

3-social-studies-us-history-exploration-.title: Exploration to Civil War
3-social-studies-us-history-exploration-.summary: Students use a timeline and map to build a simple understanding of major events from exploration through the Civil War.
objectives: Place key events in chronological order
Use a map or timeline to answer a historical question
Explain one cause-and-effect relationship from the period
3-social-studies-us-history-exploration-.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a timeline with four stops: exploration, colonies, independence, and Civil War. Ask which event came first and how students know.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how historians read timelines from left to right and maps for place clues. Introduce the idea that events happened in sequence and sometimes caused later conflicts.
outline.guided_practice: Read a simple map-timeline pair together. Identify where colonies formed and which major event came before the Civil War.
outline.independent_practice: Students answer questions using the source pair, such as which event happened earlier and what change happened over time.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students use the timeline for evidence instead of guessing from memory alone?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write one event that came before the Civil War and one clue from the timeline or map that helped them.

3-social-studies-us-history-industrializ.title: Industrialization & Immigration
3-social-studies-us-history-industrializ.summary: Students use a historical photo and an arrival checklist to learn how factories and immigration changed life in the United States.
objectives: Describe one change caused by industrialization
Describe one challenge or opportunity connected to immigration
Use details from a photo or document to support an answer
3-social-studies-us-history-industrializ.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a photo of a crowded city street or factory scene and ask what students notice about work, travel, and buildings.
outline.direct_instruction: Explain that industrialization increased factory production and changed where people worked and lived. Introduce immigration as people moving to a new country for safety, work, or opportunity.
outline.guided_practice: Study the photo and a simple arrival card together. Separate direct observations from inferences about why people moved or how cities changed.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short explanation of one way work or city life changed, using a detail from one of the sources.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students point to evidence in the source before making a claim about life in this period?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: One change I noticed was ____. The source detail that showed it was ____.

3-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.title: Ancient Civilizations
3-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.summary: Students use an artifact card and a map to learn how historians study ancient civilizations through evidence.
objectives: Identify what an artifact can tell us about a civilization
Use a map to locate an ancient civilization
Make a simple inference from source evidence
3-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a picture of an ancient tool or pot and ask what students can learn from an object without anyone telling the full story.
outline.direct_instruction: Explain that historians study artifacts and maps to learn how people lived. Model how to notice what the artifact directly shows and what can reasonably be inferred.
outline.guided_practice: Examine an artifact card and a map together. Discuss what the object may have been used for and where the civilization lived.
outline.independent_practice: Students answer questions about a second artifact or monument image, using one map clue and one artifact detail.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students separate what the source shows from what they infer about daily life?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write: This artifact suggests ____, because I noticed ____.

3-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.title: World Regions (physical/human)
3-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.summary: Students read a simple world map with landforms and people-made features to compare physical and human characteristics of regions.
objectives: Distinguish physical features from human features
Use map symbols and labels to answer questions
Describe one way people adapt to a region
3-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a world map with mountains, rivers, roads, and cities. Ask which features were made by nature and which were made by people.
outline.direct_instruction: Teach physical features as land and water forms and human features as places or structures people create. Model reading the legend and labels before answering questions.
outline.guided_practice: Use a regional map together to identify a river, a mountain range, a city, and a road. Discuss how people live differently depending on these features.
outline.independent_practice: Students read a new map and explain one physical feature, one human feature, and one way the two connect.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students use evidence from the map instead of personal guesses about the region?
outline.exit_ticket: Students name one physical feature and one human feature from the map and explain how they are different.

4-social-studies-civics-and-government-c.title: Citizenship & Rights
4-social-studies-civics-and-government-c.summary: Students use a short First Amendment excerpt and a civic scenario chart to explain how rights and responsibilities work together in a democracy.
objectives: Identify what a civic source says about a right
Explain how responsibilities help protect rights
Support a claim with evidence from a short document
4-social-studies-civics-and-government-c.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask whether everyone can say anything at any time, then show a short First Amendment excerpt and a classroom-discussion scenario.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing the excerpt by naming the document and purpose. Explain that rights protect freedoms, while responsibilities help people use those freedoms safely and fairly.
outline.guided_practice: Read the source and match it to civic scenarios involving speech, religion, and petition. Discuss which source phrases support each match.
outline.independent_practice: Students read a new community scenario and write a short claim about the right involved, using one source detail as evidence.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students connect a specific right to a scenario and explain the responsibility that keeps the community functioning?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: The source protects the right to ____. A responsibility connected to that right is ____.

4-social-studies-civics-and-government-l.title: Local/State Government
4-social-studies-civics-and-government-l.summary: Students use a powers chart and a state map to compare decisions made by local government and by state government.
objectives: Compare responsibilities of local and state government
Use a chart to classify examples correctly
Explain why one level of government fits a problem better than the other
4-social-studies-civics-and-government-l.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Present two problems: repairing a city playground and setting statewide driver's license rules. Ask which one sounds local and which sounds state-level.
outline.direct_instruction: Teach students to use headings and categories in a powers chart. Model how place, scale, and number of people affected can help decide which level of government acts.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze a chart of local and state responsibilities together. Compare how each level serves people in different ways.
outline.independent_practice: Students read four civic problems, classify them as local or state, and justify their answers with chart evidence.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students explain their reasoning instead of only naming the correct level?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write one example of a local government job and one example of a state government job, including one reason for each.

4-social-studies-economics-and-financial.title: Markets (intro)
4-social-studies-economics-and-financial.summary: Students read a market price table and a short news blurb to explain how markets respond when buyers and sellers meet.
objectives: Describe how buyers and sellers interact in a market
Use a table to compare prices and quantities
Explain how a market source helps answer an economic question
4-social-studies-economics-and-financial.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a table of fruit prices before and after a rainy week and ask why one item might now cost more.
outline.direct_instruction: Review markets as places where buying and selling happen. Model how to read a market table and connect changes in price to what the source suggests about supply or demand.
outline.guided_practice: Examine the table and short market blurb together. Identify direct observations first, then discuss what those details may mean.
outline.independent_practice: Students analyze a second market example and explain what buyers and sellers might do based on the source.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students use numbers and source details to support an economic explanation?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: One thing I observed in the market source was ____. That matters because ____.

4-social-studies-economics-and-financial.title: Producers/Consumers
4-social-studies-economics-and-financial.summary: Students use a supply-chain card set and a receipt to trace how goods move from producers to consumers.
objectives: Identify producers and consumers in a chain of exchange
Explain how a good moves from creation to purchase
Use source details to support an economic explanation
4-social-studies-economics-and-financial.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask how a T-shirt reaches a store shelf. Show a tag, shipping label, and receipt to start the investigation.
outline.direct_instruction: Teach that producers may grow, build, transport, or sell goods before consumers buy them. Model how to read source clues that show each step in the chain.
outline.guided_practice: Put card-set events in order together and discuss which people are acting as producers and which as consumers.
outline.independent_practice: Students trace a new product from producer to consumer and explain one part of the path using evidence from the source cards.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students explain how roles connect across a sequence rather than treating each role separately?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: In this chain, the producer was ____, the consumer was ____, and the evidence was ____.

4-social-studies-us-history-exploration-.title: Exploration to Civil War
4-social-studies-us-history-exploration-.summary: Students use a map, timeline, and short diary excerpt to explain how expansion and conflict changed the United States before the Civil War.
objectives: Use chronology to place major events in order
Identify one historical change shown in a map or source
Support a simple cause-and-effect claim with evidence
4-social-studies-us-history-exploration-.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show an early United States map beside a later one and ask what changed over time.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to read a map for territorial change and a timeline for sequence. Introduce the idea that land expansion and disagreements about power and slavery increased conflict.
outline.guided_practice: Read the paired sources together and identify what is directly shown, what changed, and what students can infer from that change.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short explanation of one event or change that helped lead toward the Civil War, citing one timeline or map detail.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students distinguish between a direct observation from the map and an inference about its impact?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: One change over time was ____. I know because the source showed ____.

4-social-studies-us-history-industrializ.title: Industrialization & Immigration
4-social-studies-us-history-industrializ.summary: Students examine a factory photo and an immigration checkpoint document to explain how work and migration reshaped American cities.
objectives: Describe one feature of industrial work
Describe one reason people immigrated
Use evidence from a visual or document to support a historical claim
4-social-studies-us-history-industrializ.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Display a factory photo and ask what clues show this is a different kind of workplace from a farm.
outline.direct_instruction: Explain industrialization as a shift toward machine-based production and immigration as movement to a new country for opportunity or safety. Model sourcing the photo and document.
outline.guided_practice: Identify direct details in the sources such as crowding, machines, luggage, signs, and checkpoints. Discuss what those observations suggest.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short response explaining how industrialization or immigration changed daily life, using one source detail.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students support a claim about change with actual evidence instead of only background knowledge?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: This source suggests that life was changing because ____.

4-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.title: Ancient Civilizations
4-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.summary: Students use a map and two artifact images to explain how location and evidence help historians study ancient civilizations.
objectives: Locate an ancient civilization on a map
Describe what an artifact reveals about daily life
Use source evidence to make a supported inference
4-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a picture of a clay tablet and ask how one object can help historians learn about a whole civilization.
outline.direct_instruction: Teach students to look for place clues on a map and use artifact features as evidence of work, trade, writing, or belief. Model the difference between observation and inference.
outline.guided_practice: Study a river-valley map and artifact cards together. Discuss why location near water mattered and what the artifacts suggest about civilization life.
outline.independent_practice: Students analyze a second artifact and write a supported inference about the people who used it.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students use both map context and artifact evidence in the same explanation?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: This artifact helps me infer that ____ because I noticed ____.

4-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.title: World Regions (physical/human)
4-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.summary: Students use a thematic map and a settlement chart to compare physical and human characteristics of world regions.
objectives: Identify physical and human features in a region
Explain how physical features affect where people live
Use map evidence to compare regions
4-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask why many cities grow near rivers or coasts, then show a map where settlements cluster around water.
outline.direct_instruction: Review how physical features shape movement, farming, and settlement. Model reading a thematic map and using a chart to compare regions.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze a map and settlement chart together. Identify one pattern students can see directly and one reasonable interpretation.
outline.independent_practice: Students compare two regions using source evidence about climate, landforms, and human activity.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students explain how a physical feature influences a human feature with evidence from the map?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: In this region, people likely live near ____ because the map shows ____.

5-social-studies-civics-and-government-c.title: Citizenship & Rights
5-social-studies-civics-and-government-c.summary: Students use a Bill of Rights excerpt and a petition scenario to explain how civic rights are protected and practiced.
objectives: Identify a right protected in a founding document
Explain how citizens can use rights responsibly
Support a civic claim with source evidence
5-social-studies-civics-and-government-c.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Pose the question: How can people disagree with a government decision without losing their rights? Introduce a short petition scenario.
outline.direct_instruction: Source a Bill of Rights excerpt and model how to connect document language to a modern civic situation. Emphasize claim, evidence, and reasoning.
outline.guided_practice: Read the source together and match it to petition, assembly, speech, and press scenarios. Discuss which words from the document matter most.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short paragraph explaining which right is involved in a civic scenario and what responsibility should guide its use.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students support their claim with exact source details rather than broad opinions about fairness?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: The right in this case is ____. My evidence is ____. A responsible action would be ____.

5-social-studies-civics-and-government-l.title: Local/State Government
5-social-studies-civics-and-government-l.summary: Students use a powers chart and a sample budget problem to compare how local and state governments make decisions.
objectives: Compare the responsibilities of local and state government
Use a chart to classify government actions
Explain which level of government should address a specific issue
5-social-studies-civics-and-government-l.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask whether fixing a city bus route and funding statewide highways should be decided by the same government level.
outline.direct_instruction: Explain that local and state governments have different scopes, budgets, and responsibilities. Model how to read a powers chart and use evidence from a public-spending scenario.
outline.guided_practice: Compare several examples together and discuss which level is best positioned to act and why.
outline.independent_practice: Students read a short civic problem and defend whether local or state government should lead the response, using source evidence.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students explain how scale and responsibility shape government action?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write one issue for local government and one for state government, with one source-based reason for each.

5-social-studies-economics-and-financial.title: Markets (intro)
5-social-studies-economics-and-financial.summary: Students use a market table and a short article about changing prices to explain how markets react when conditions change.
objectives: Explain how buyers and sellers influence a market
Interpret a simple market table
Use source evidence to explain a price change
5-social-studies-economics-and-financial.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a before-and-after price table for strawberries and ask what may have caused the difference.
outline.direct_instruction: Review markets as systems where buyers and sellers meet. Model how to connect evidence from a table and article to a claim about why a price changed.
outline.guided_practice: Read the paired sources together, first noting direct observations, then building a simple explanation.
outline.independent_practice: Students analyze a second market example and write a short response about what the source suggests happened.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students use both numeric and written evidence in the same explanation?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: The price changed because ____. I know because the source showed ____.

5-social-studies-economics-and-financial.title: Producers/Consumers
5-social-studies-economics-and-financial.summary: Students use an advertisement, product tag, and receipt to explain how producers and consumers affect each other.
objectives: Identify producer and consumer roles in a market exchange
Explain how producers try to attract consumers
Support an economic claim with source evidence
5-social-studies-economics-and-financial.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a cereal advertisement beside a store receipt and ask which source was made to convince someone to buy.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to source an advertisement for purpose and audience. Explain how producers create, market, and sell goods while consumers decide what to buy.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze the sources together and discuss how producer choices may affect consumer behavior.
outline.independent_practice: Students explain how one source shows a producer's goal and how a consumer responds, using details from the evidence.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students explain the relationship between producer decisions and consumer choices?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: The producer wanted consumers to ____. I know because ____.

5-social-studies-us-history-exploration-.title: Exploration to Civil War
5-social-studies-us-history-exploration-.summary: Students use a map, timeline, and short source set to explain how expansion, conflict, and sectional differences led toward the Civil War.
objectives: Place key events in order and context
Identify one cause of growing sectional conflict
Use evidence from multiple sources to support a claim
5-social-studies-us-history-exploration-.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a westward-expansion map and ask why adding land could create political conflict.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to combine a timeline, map, and short excerpt to understand cause and effect. Explain that the same event can matter differently to different groups.
outline.guided_practice: Work through the source set together and identify one direct observation from each source before building an explanation.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short claim about a cause of sectional conflict and support it with evidence from at least two sources.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students use source evidence to connect expansion and disagreement instead of treating events as isolated facts?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: One cause of conflict was ____. My evidence is ____ and ____.

5-social-studies-us-history-industrializ.title: Industrialization & Immigration
5-social-studies-us-history-industrializ.summary: Students use a photo, short testimony, and arrival record to explain how industrial growth and immigration changed cities and work.
objectives: Describe one major change caused by industrialization
Explain one reason immigrants came to the United States
Support a historical interpretation with source evidence
5-social-studies-us-history-industrializ.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask whether a growing city offered only opportunity or also created new problems. Display a city-tenement or factory image.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing a photo and short testimony. Explain how industrial growth created jobs, crowded cities, and difficult working conditions for many families.
outline.guided_practice: Read the sources together, separating direct observation from inference and discussing perspective.
outline.independent_practice: Students explain one opportunity and one challenge from the period, citing at least one source detail.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students account for source perspective instead of assuming one source tells the whole story?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write: This source suggests that industrialization or immigration changed life by ____.

5-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.title: Ancient Civilizations
5-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.summary: Students compare maps and artifacts to explain how environment, belief, and innovation shaped ancient civilizations.
objectives: Use map evidence to explain why civilizations developed in certain places
Compare what different artifacts reveal about society
Support a historical inference with source evidence
5-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show artifacts from two ancient civilizations and ask what students can infer about work, writing, or belief from each object.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to compare artifact evidence and map context. Emphasize that historians build claims from patterns across multiple sources.
outline.guided_practice: Study a river-valley map and artifact pair together. Discuss how environment may have supported farming, trade, or settlement.
outline.independent_practice: Students compare two sources and write a short claim about how one civilization adapted or organized life.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students use source comparison rather than only listing facts about a civilization?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: I infer that this civilization valued ____ because the sources show ____.

5-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.title: World Regions (physical/human)
5-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.summary: Students use physical and human geography maps to explain how landforms, climate, and human activity shape world regions.
objectives: Interpret physical and human geography maps together
Explain how geography influences settlement and activity
Use map evidence to compare regions
5-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show two maps of the same region, one physical and one human, and ask why people often build cities in certain places.
outline.direct_instruction: Model comparing map types and using them for a claim about movement, farming, trade, or settlement. Emphasize evidence before explanation.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze the paired maps together and identify one physical feature and one human pattern that seem connected.
outline.independent_practice: Students compare two world regions and explain how geography affects how people live in each place.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students point to map evidence when explaining why people settled or traveled in a region?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write: In this region, geography matters because ____.

6-social-studies-civics-and-government-e.title: Elections & Civic Participation
6-social-studies-civics-and-government-e.summary: Students analyze a sample ballot and voter-turnout chart to explain how elections work and why participation matters.
objectives: Identify what a ballot and turnout chart directly show
Explain one reason civic participation matters
Support a claim with evidence from an election source
6-social-studies-civics-and-government-e.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a sample ballot and ask what information a voter needs before making a choice.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing a ballot and turnout chart, noticing office names, measures, and participation patterns. Explain that civic participation includes voting and other actions that shape public life.
outline.guided_practice: Read the sources together and separate direct observations from interpretations about participation.
outline.independent_practice: Students explain why turnout matters in a democracy, using one detail from the chart or ballot.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students move from source observation to a supported civic claim without drifting into unsupported opinion?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: One thing the election source shows is ____. This matters because ____.

6-social-studies-civics-and-government-u.title: US Constitution & Federalism
6-social-studies-civics-and-government-u.summary: Students analyze a short constitutional excerpt and a powers chart to explain how federalism divides power.
objectives: Identify powers described in a constitutional source
Explain the idea of shared and separate powers
Use source evidence to justify a federalism claim
6-social-studies-civics-and-government-u.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask who should decide school requirements, the national government or the states, then introduce a constitutional powers chart.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing a constitutional excerpt and using a powers chart to distinguish federal, state, and shared powers. Explain why federalism exists.
outline.guided_practice: Work through examples together and cite chart evidence for each level of government.
outline.independent_practice: Students analyze a new issue and explain which level of government has the strongest role, using source evidence.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students explain how the chart supports their claim instead of only naming a level of government?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: Federalism means ____. One source detail that shows this is ____.

6-social-studies-economics-and-financial.title: Personal Finance Basics
6-social-studies-economics-and-financial.summary: Students analyze a simple weekly budget and spending log to explain needs, wants, saving, and trade-offs.
objectives: Read a basic budget source
Distinguish needs from wants in a spending plan
Use evidence from a budget to explain a financial choice
6-social-studies-economics-and-financial.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a weekly budget with cash in and planned spending out. Ask which items students would protect first if money ran short.
outline.direct_instruction: Model reading the budget categories and identifying needs, wants, and savings choices. Explain that financial decisions involve trade-offs.
outline.guided_practice: Use the spending log together to decide which choices match the budget and which create problems.
outline.independent_practice: Students explain one smart revision to the budget and support it with evidence from the source.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students use the numbers and categories in the budget to justify a recommendation?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write: I would change ____ in this budget because ____.

6-social-studies-economics-and-financial.title: Supply/Demand (intro)
6-social-studies-economics-and-financial.summary: Students analyze a price-and-quantity chart to explain how supply and demand can affect market outcomes.
objectives: Describe what supply and demand mean in a source-based context
Interpret a basic market chart
Use evidence from the chart to explain a price change
6-social-studies-economics-and-financial.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask why concert tickets might cost more when many people want them and only a few are available.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to read a simple chart showing quantity and price. Explain supply as how much sellers offer and demand as how much buyers want.
outline.guided_practice: Read the chart together and identify what students can directly observe before building an interpretation.
outline.independent_practice: Students explain a market change using one chart detail and one sentence about supply or demand.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students connect evidence from the chart to the correct economic idea?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: The price changed because ____. My evidence is ____.

6-social-studies-us-history-postwar-to-p.title: Postwar to Present
6-social-studies-us-history-postwar-to-p.summary: Students analyze a timeline and public message source to explain how life in the United States changed after World War II.
objectives: Place key postwar developments in order
Identify one theme of change in a public message source
Support a continuity-or-change claim with evidence
6-social-studies-us-history-postwar-to-p.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a postwar advertisement or public image and ask what it suggests about hopes or priorities in the period.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing the image and reading a timeline for major developments such as suburban growth, Cold War tension, or rights movements.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze the paired sources together and identify one direct observation and one supported inference.
outline.independent_practice: Students explain one way the postwar period changed American life, using evidence from the source set.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students use timeline context to interpret the public message source accurately?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: One important change after World War II was ____. The source detail that supports this is ____.

6-social-studies-us-history-reconstructi.title: Reconstruction to WWII
6-social-studies-us-history-reconstructi.summary: Students analyze a timeline and historical document excerpt to explain how the United States changed from Reconstruction through World War II.
objectives: Identify a major development from Reconstruction to WWII
Use source evidence to explain change over time
Support a claim with a document detail and timeline context
6-social-studies-us-history-reconstructi.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask how one country could experience constitutional change, industrial growth, and global war in the same broad era.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to read the period as a sequence of linked changes. Source the excerpt and explain how document evidence fits into the larger timeline.
outline.guided_practice: Work through the sources together and identify a turning point students can support with evidence.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short explanation of one major change in this era and support it with the source set.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students connect a document detail to a broader historical development?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write: A turning point in this era was ____. I know because ____.

6-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.title: Global Interactions
6-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.summary: Students analyze a trade-route map and short case study to explain how ideas, goods, and people move across regions.
objectives: Interpret a map of global interaction
Explain one effect of movement between regions
Use source evidence to support a claim about exchange
6-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a trade-route map and ask what might travel along the same route besides products.
outline.direct_instruction: Teach students to look for direction, connection points, and regions on the map. Explain that interactions can spread goods, ideas, technologies, and disease.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze the map and case study together, separating direct observations from inferences about effects.
outline.independent_practice: Students explain one effect of global interaction using evidence from the map or case study.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students support their claim with actual route or source details?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: This interaction changed regions by ____.

6-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.title: Medieval to Early Modern
6-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.summary: Students analyze a map and short source excerpt to explain how trade, belief, and power changed during the medieval and early modern periods.
objectives: Use map evidence to identify a historical connection
Interpret a short source in context
Support a claim about change over time with evidence
6-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Display a Silk Road or Mediterranean trade map and ask how connected regions might influence one another.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing a short historical excerpt and pairing it with a map for context. Explain how trade, faith, and political power shaped the era.
outline.guided_practice: Read the sources together and identify one direct observation from each before building an interpretation.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short claim about how contact between regions changed societies in this era, using evidence from the map or text.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students ground their historical claim in source evidence instead of broad summary alone?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: One important connection in this era was ____. The evidence is ____.

7-social-studies-civics-and-government-e.title: Elections & Civic Participation
7-social-studies-civics-and-government-e.summary: Students analyze a voter-turnout chart, issue summary, and sample ballot to explain how civic participation shapes representation.
objectives: Interpret what election sources directly show
Explain how participation affects representation
Support a civic argument with chart or document evidence
7-social-studies-civics-and-government-e.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask whether an election result tells the whole story if turnout is low, then show a turnout chart beside the result.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing the ballot and turnout chart and discussing what each source can and cannot prove. Explain how participation shapes whose voices are represented.
outline.guided_practice: Read the sources together and distinguish direct observation, inference, and opinion.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short claim about why participation matters and support it with evidence from the election sources.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students use turnout evidence carefully without making unsupported assumptions about voter motives?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: Participation matters because ____. My best evidence is ____.

7-social-studies-civics-and-government-u.title: US Constitution & Federalism
7-social-studies-civics-and-government-u.summary: Students analyze constitutional excerpts and a federal-state powers chart to explain how federalism can create both balance and conflict.
objectives: Identify powers assigned to different levels of government
Explain how federalism structures decision-making
Use constitutional evidence to support a claim
7-social-studies-civics-and-government-u.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Present a current-style issue such as disaster response or school policy and ask which level of government should lead.
outline.direct_instruction: Source the constitutional excerpt and powers chart. Model how to distinguish enumerated, reserved, and shared powers and how those categories affect real disputes.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze several examples together and support each answer with chart or document evidence.
outline.independent_practice: Students argue which level of government has the strongest role in a new case and justify the claim with source evidence.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students explain why the Constitution creates overlap in some cases rather than assuming every power is separate?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write: Federalism creates balance because ____.

7-social-studies-economics-and-financial.title: Personal Finance Basics
7-social-studies-economics-and-financial.summary: Students analyze a paycheck, budget, and savings goal to explain how personal finance choices involve trade-offs and planning.
objectives: Interpret a basic pay or budget source
Explain how saving and spending choices affect goals
Support a financial recommendation with evidence
7-social-studies-economics-and-financial.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a sample paycheck and ask why the amount earned is not always the same as the amount available to spend.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to read the paycheck and budget lines. Explain trade-offs, savings goals, and why financial planning depends on evidence, not impulse.
outline.guided_practice: Work through a sample budget together and identify where the plan supports or harms a savings goal.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short recommendation for how to revise the budget, using the source for evidence.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students justify a budgeting choice with numeric evidence and a clear reason?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: To reach the savings goal, I would change ____ because ____.

7-social-studies-economics-and-financial.title: Supply/Demand (intro)
7-social-studies-economics-and-financial.summary: Students analyze a market graph and short scenario to explain how changes in supply or demand affect price and quantity.
objectives: Interpret what a market graph directly shows
Explain whether a scenario changes supply or demand
Support an economic explanation with evidence
7-social-studies-economics-and-financial.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask why snow shovels can become expensive before a big storm and which part of the market likely changed.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to read a simple supply-demand graph and pair it with a scenario. Emphasize evidence from the graph before explanation.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze the graph and scenario together, identifying the direct pattern and the most likely explanation.
outline.independent_practice: Students explain a second market case with one graph detail and one source-based reason.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students avoid mixing up what the graph shows with what they infer caused it?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: This scenario changed ____, and I know because ____.

7-social-studies-us-history-postwar-to-p.title: Postwar to Present
7-social-studies-us-history-postwar-to-p.summary: Students analyze a timeline and public argument source to explain how postwar change affected rights, policy, and daily life.
objectives: Identify a major postwar development in context
Interpret a public message source for perspective and purpose
Support a claim about continuity or change with evidence
7-social-studies-us-history-postwar-to-p.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a public speech excerpt or poster from the period and ask whose perspective it represents.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing the message and using a timeline to place it in context. Emphasize observation, perspective, and supported inference.
outline.guided_practice: Work through the paired sources together and discuss what the source directly says, what it suggests, and why the context matters.
outline.independent_practice: Students explain one way the postwar era changed public life, using evidence from the timeline and message source.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students connect source perspective to broader historical change without overgeneralizing?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: This source reflects change in ____ because ____.

7-social-studies-us-history-reconstructi.title: Reconstruction to WWII
7-social-studies-us-history-reconstructi.summary: Students analyze a timeline, political cartoon, and short document excerpt to explain key tensions from Reconstruction through World War II.
objectives: Interpret a source from within its historical context
Explain one major tension or turning point from the era
Support a historical claim with evidence from multiple sources
7-social-studies-us-history-reconstructi.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a political cartoon from the era and ask what problem or debate it seems to highlight.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing the cartoon and document, then using the timeline to place the issue in context. Emphasize that source perspective shapes meaning.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze what the sources directly show and what conclusions students can responsibly draw.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short claim about a turning point or tension in the era and support it with evidence from at least two sources.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students use context to interpret a political cartoon instead of reading it only literally?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: A major tension in this era was ____. My evidence is ____.

7-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.title: Global Interactions
7-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.summary: Students analyze a migration or trade map and supporting source to explain how global interactions spread change across regions.
objectives: Interpret a source showing regional interaction
Explain one consequence of global exchange
Support a claim about interaction with evidence
7-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask how an invention, belief, or disease can travel farther than the people who first created or encountered it.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to read a map of movement and pair it with a short case-study source. Emphasize evidence, context, and consequence.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze the sources together and discuss one positive and one negative effect students can support with evidence.
outline.independent_practice: Students explain one major effect of global interaction using details from the map and source.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students trace an interaction across regions with evidence instead of vague summary?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write: This interaction mattered because ____.

7-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.title: Medieval to Early Modern
7-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.summary: Students analyze a map and document excerpt to explain how trade, reform, and state power reshaped societies from the medieval to early modern era.
objectives: Interpret a historical map or document in context
Explain one major transformation from the era
Support a claim about change with evidence
7-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a map of trade or political boundaries and ask what it reveals about power and connection in the era.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing the document and using the map for context. Explain that trade, belief, and political centralization all changed societies over time.
outline.guided_practice: Read the source pair together and identify one direct observation from each before building an interpretation.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short response explaining one transformation in the era with evidence from the map or document.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students connect a specific source detail to a broader historical transformation?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: One important transformation was ____. The evidence is ____.

8-social-studies-civics-and-government-e.title: Elections & Civic Participation
8-social-studies-civics-and-government-e.summary: Students analyze a turnout chart, campaign message, and ballot source to explain how participation, information, and persuasion affect democratic decision-making.
objectives: Interpret election sources for evidence and purpose
Explain how participation shapes democratic outcomes
Support a civic claim with multiple sources
8-social-studies-civics-and-government-e.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask whether a campaign message and a turnout chart tell the same story about an election, then display both sources.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing the campaign message for purpose and audience and the turnout chart for direct evidence. Explain how information, persuasion, and participation all affect representation.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze the sources together and distinguish observation, inference, and evaluation carefully.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short claim about why participation matters in elections and support it with evidence from two sources.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students account for source purpose without confusing persuasion with factual evidence?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: In a democracy, participation matters because ____. My evidence is ____.

8-social-studies-civics-and-government-u.title: US Constitution & Federalism
8-social-studies-civics-and-government-u.summary: Students analyze constitutional text and a federal-state powers case to explain how federalism distributes authority and creates debate.
objectives: Interpret constitutional evidence about government power
Explain how federalism can balance and complicate decision-making
Support a constitutional claim with source evidence
8-social-studies-civics-and-government-u.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Present a modern-style policy conflict and ask why the Constitution does not always produce a simple one-level answer.
outline.direct_instruction: Source the constitutional text and case summary. Model how to use the sources to explain why authority may be shared, disputed, or limited.
outline.guided_practice: Work through the case together and identify which source details support different interpretations.
outline.independent_practice: Students argue which level of government has the stronger claim in a new scenario and justify the answer with source evidence.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students explain constitutional reasoning with evidence instead of defaulting to opinion about what should happen?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: Federalism creates debate when ____. One source detail that supports this is ____.

8-social-studies-economics-and-financial.title: Personal Finance Basics
8-social-studies-economics-and-financial.summary: Students analyze a budget, bank-fee table, and savings goal to explain how financial choices affect long-term outcomes.
objectives: Interpret multiple personal-finance sources
Explain how fees, spending, and saving affect a plan
Support a financial recommendation with evidence
8-social-studies-economics-and-financial.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show two banking choices with different fees and ask how a small difference can matter over time.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to read a budget and fee table together. Explain that strong financial decisions come from comparing evidence and thinking about long-term goals.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze the sources together and identify which plan better supports the stated goal and why.
outline.independent_practice: Students recommend a financial choice and support it with details from the sources.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students connect short-term spending choices to long-term outcomes using evidence?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write: The better financial choice is ____, because ____.

8-social-studies-economics-and-financial.title: Supply/Demand (intro)
8-social-studies-economics-and-financial.summary: Students analyze a market graph and policy or event scenario to explain how supply and demand influence outcomes in real markets.
objectives: Interpret a market graph in context
Explain how a scenario affects supply or demand
Support an economic claim with graph evidence
8-social-studies-economics-and-financial.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask why a shortage or policy change can alter prices quickly even when the product itself stays the same.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to read the graph for direct evidence and then connect that evidence to the scenario. Emphasize careful explanation rather than memorized vocabulary alone.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze one graph-scenario pair together and discuss both the direct pattern and the likely cause.
outline.independent_practice: Students explain a second market case with evidence from the graph and scenario.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students use graph evidence precisely instead of offering a generic explanation about prices?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: This market changed because ____. My evidence is ____.

8-social-studies-us-history-postwar-to-p.title: Postwar to Present
8-social-studies-us-history-postwar-to-p.summary: Students analyze a timeline and argument source to explain how policy, rights, and public debate shaped the United States from the postwar era to the present.
objectives: Interpret a postwar source for perspective and purpose
Explain one major change or continuity from the era
Support a historical claim with evidence from multiple sources
8-social-studies-us-history-postwar-to-p.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Display a protest poster, speech excerpt, or public announcement and ask what issue seems urgent to the source creator.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing the argument source and using the timeline to place it in context. Emphasize perspective, purpose, and evidence-based interpretation.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze what the source directly says, what it implies, and which timeline details help explain why it was created.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short claim about one major postwar development and support it with evidence from the source set.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students connect source perspective to historical change without ignoring limits or bias?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: This source matters because it reveals ____ about the postwar era.

8-social-studies-us-history-reconstructi.title: Reconstruction to WWII
8-social-studies-us-history-reconstructi.summary: Students analyze a timeline, public message source, and short document to explain how the United States changed from Reconstruction through World War II.
objectives: Identify a major turning point in the era
Interpret source evidence in historical context
Support a claim about change and continuity with evidence
8-social-studies-us-history-reconstructi.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Ask how one era can include both expanded rights and serious inequality, then display a source that reflects one side of that tension.
outline.direct_instruction: Model using the timeline for context and the document for evidence. Emphasize that historians must account for perspective, purpose, and historical limits.
outline.guided_practice: Read the source set together and identify one direct observation, one reasonable inference, and one supported claim.
outline.independent_practice: Students explain one major turning point or tension from the era using evidence from the document and timeline.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students build a disciplined claim instead of summarizing the era too broadly?
outline.exit_ticket: Students write: A key turning point in this era was ____. My evidence is ____.

8-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.title: Global Interactions
8-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.summary: Students analyze a map and short case study to explain how globalization connects regions through trade, migration, and shared problems.
objectives: Interpret source evidence about global interaction
Explain one consequence of globalization
Support a claim with map or document evidence
8-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show a product journey map or migration route and ask how one decision in one region can affect people in another.
outline.direct_instruction: Model how to use the map for direct evidence and the case study for context and consequence. Emphasize that global interactions can create both opportunity and tension.
outline.guided_practice: Analyze the sources together and identify one direct effect and one broader implication students can support.
outline.independent_practice: Students write a short explanation of one way globalization connects regions, using source evidence.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students support their globalization claim with concrete evidence instead of broad slogans?
outline.exit_ticket: Students answer: This global interaction mattered because ____.

8-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.title: Medieval to Early Modern
8-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.summary: Students analyze a map and historical excerpt to explain how trade, reform, and expanding state power transformed societies from the medieval to early modern era.
objectives: Interpret historical evidence from the era in context
Explain one major transformation from medieval to early modern times
Support a claim with map and document evidence
8-social-studies-world-history-and-geogr.subject: Social Studies

outline.hook: Show changing political or trade maps and ask what they suggest about growing state power and wider connections.
outline.direct_instruction: Model sourcing the excerpt and pairing it with the map to explain context. Emphasize how reform, trade, and state-building changed societies over time.
outline.guided_practice: Read the source pair together and distinguish what each source directly shows from what students infer about larger change.
outline.independent_practice: Students argue for one major transformation in the era and support it with evidence from both sources.
outline.check_for_understanding: Can students build a supported interpretation from multiple sources instead of treating the era as one simple story?
outline.exit_ticket: Students complete: One transformation in this era was ____. The evidence is ____ and ____.
